title: Painting underglaze on leather-hard stoneware
subtitle: Applying coloured underglaze to greenware before bisque firing
excerpt: Pre-mixed underglazes are painted directly onto leather-hard stoneware greenware, layered to build colour depth, and then covered with a clear glaze coat after bisque firing. The underglaze colour is stable through both firings and does not move during the glaze firing.
sourceNotes: Underglaze on greenware painting technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A coloured ceramic pigment applied to greenware or bisqueware before a clear or transparent glaze coat. Underglazes are stable through firing and produce reliable, repeatable colours.
A clay drying stage where the piece is firm enough to decorate without distorting, but still slightly damp so the underglaze can bond to the surface.
A first low-temperature kiln firing that hardens greenware without glaze. The piece emerges as porous bisqueware, ready for glazing.
A transparent glaze applied over underglaze decoration to seal and protect it and to give the finished piece a smooth, glassy surface.

content.text: stage bonds directly to the clay surface and survives both the
and the subsequent glaze firing intact. Pre-mixed underglazes come in a wide range of colours and can be layered, blended at their edges while wet, or applied in fine detail lines. A
coat over the bisqued piece is the standard finish.

attrs.content: Use pre-mixed commercial underglazes only. Wear an N95 mask when applying underglaze and throughout kiln work. Clean up wet — never dry sweep ceramic dust. Dedicate all brushes and containers to ceramic use only.

items.name: Pottery brush, mop (for washes and backgrounds)

content.text: Place the leather-hard piece on the banding wheel. Stir the underglaze to an even consistency. It should brush smoothly without being watery.

Apply the first colour in broad areas using the mop brush. Two coats give more saturated colour than one; apply each coat in a different direction (horizontal then vertical) and allow the first coat to dry before applying the second.
Add detail and line work with the liner brush. Underglaze behaves like thick watercolour. It dries quickly on the porous greenware surface, so work confidently and avoid scrubbing over dried areas.
Allow the decorated piece to continue drying slowly. Rapid drying on top of underglaze can cause the underglaze layer to peel off at the dry-greenware stage.

After bisque firing, the underglaze colours will appear slightly paler and more matt than they will in the final firing. This is normal. Apply a clear glaze coat by dipping, brushing, or pouring. The clear glaze seals and brightens the underglaze colours. Fire to the glaze temperature recommended for the clay body and glaze.
Apply underglaze to bisqueware instead of greenware. You get more working time and can fix mistakes more easily.
Sketch the design onto the leather-hard surface first. A pencil or sgraffito tool works well. Then apply the underglaze over the sketch.

items.symptom: Underglaze peels off in dry-greenware stage before bisque firing
items.cause: Piece dried too rapidly after painting; underglaze did not bond fully
items.fix: After painting, cover the piece loosely with plastic to slow the drying rate for the first few hours

items.symptom: Colours appear washed out and pale in the final fired piece
items.cause: Single underglaze coat only; not enough pigment density
items.fix: Apply two to three coats of each underglaze colour, allowing each coat to dry before the next
